people across Aotearoa. It's an attempt to provide unrealistic
certainty, particularly around the continuation of funding for family
carers. However, it does so by significantly weakening
rights, protections, and accountability.
Whanau supports need to be backed by a fair and just system that
provides all the support needed for not only disabled people but also
their carers. The overall direction of this proposed bill is not
supportive of disabled people and should not proceed.
A central and deeply concerning feature of the bill is the removal of
access to established complaint mechanisms, specifically the Human
Rights Commission and the Health and Disability Commissioner.
This effectively removes independent avenues for redress without
providing any clear or equivalent alternative.
This is absolutely unjust. It strips disabled people of a key
protection and leaves a significant gap in
accountability.
If a disabled person experiences poor treatment, discrimination,
or harm under the system created by this proposed bill, there is
no clear path to justice, support, or legal representation.
Disabled people will be left without adequate and essential
protection. This proposed bill fails to adequately
recognize or address the risks that can exist within family
care situations. While many whanau provide high-quality, culturally
appropriate, and deeply committed care, not all environments
are safe or supportive. There are circumstances where
neglect, coercion, or misuse of funding can occur.
In removing external complaint pathways while also expanding reliance on
family-based care, the bill does not demonstrate how disabled people
will be safeguarded in situations where care arrangements
are not working or are actively harmful.
Without a clear independent mechanism for monitoring intervention or
advocacy, this creates an unacceptable level of risk for
some of the most vulnerable people in our communities.
Through lack of meaningful engagement of disabled people, this proposed
bill undermines trust and is inconsistent with the
principles of participation and partnership.
An emphasis on fiscal risk and cost containment, including a
suggestion that increased funding may not be feasible or access to
disability support services may need to be reduced, signals a
shift away from a rights-based approach.
The use of a three-year transition
period provides
absolutely no reassurance on outcomes.
There is insufficient
clarity about what any long-term system will look like.
There is a real risk supports will diminish, protections weaken even further,
and disabled people will continue to face increased uncertainty.
Asset or means testing should not be a consideration in
disability support
because it ignores the real needs of disabled people and creates
another unfair and unjust barrier.
Introducing such significant legislative change without a clearly
defined and rights-based end state is not acceptable.
The bill is not fit for purpose. There has been no allowance for
people to think it through, talk with others, and provide
meaningful feedback.
Where is the co-design? Where is the thorough and engaged consultation
with disabled people informed by the lived experience, grounded in
rights, dignity, and inclusion? I sincerely ask

here, because real consultation takes time.
Time to understand, time to think, time to respond.
Legislation must be co-designed.
Disabled people and their whanau and supporters need to be fully involved,
fully informed, and genuinely listened to by the
decision-makers,
not after the fact, but as part of shaping
what happens next.

Yeah. Well, I've got a lot to say and a lot of time, so I'll be direct about why
this bill is concerning. The Supreme Court's 2025 ruling opened a pathway for
family carers to seek employment status and rights for the disability support they
provide. This bill closes that pathway.
It extinguishes the 40-plus claims already in progress, blocks any future
employment-related claims, and bars carers and disabled people from bringing
related complaints to Employment Relations Authority, the Employment Court, the
Human Rights Commission, or the Health and Disability Commissioner.
This is not a minor technical fix. It is Parliament overriding a court decision
specifically to stop carers from being recognized and paid as employees.
Legislation that permanently forecloses a court-recognized legal pathway for tens
of thousands of disabled whānau carers deserves a genuinely co-designed, well-being
centered process, not fast track.
I'll focus on family carers and what losing this pathway means for them, for the
people they care for, and for the country's books.
Carers are already underpaid and overworked, propping up an overburdened healthcare
system. Closing off their path to employment rights locks in more unpaid and
unrecognized care, which is dangerous for both carer and disabled person.
Rana Itel's 2026 study, applying theory of caregiver stress to 15 years of
Australian panel data, found carers reported higher happiness than non-carers
when providing under six hours of care a week, but lower happiness once care
exceeded 11 hours a week. The highest burden group, who provided 20-plus hours
weekly, showed the clearest decline in well-being.
This bill, by foreclosing the path to paid recognized work, pushes more carers into
that high hour, low recognition bracket rather than out of it.
Australian research backs this up.
Carers of family members with severe illnesses are more likely to have reduced paid
employment, higher out-of-pocket costs, and greater exposure to economic decline
and financial stress. Underpaid carers also carry a heavier cognitive load,
which compounds poor well-being, meaning stressed, fatigued carers and the people
they care for are both more vulnerable to accidents.
Carers in this position have neither the time nor the resources to look after their
own health. This bill is being sold as fiscal restraint, but Infometrics
2022 analysis for Alzheimer's New Zealand, Carers New Zealand, the Carers
Alliance, IHC, and MSD shows that restraint is an excuse, not a
saving. In this, they noted that one in seven New Zealand adults,
14%, is an unpaid carer. Additionally, there are already
7.9 unpaid carers for every practicing nurse and 9.6 for
every personal care assistant or disability support worker.
A bill that entrenches unpaid family labor as the default rather than a recognized
employment relationship will push that ratio higher still.
Infometrics estimates carers lose between 1.5 and 2.3
billion in employment revenue annually from reduced hours and lost paid work,
and that the government loses 540 to 769
million a year in tax revenue as a direct result.
By legislating away the Crown's status as employer, this government isn't avoiding
the cost. It's choosing to keep it sitting with families instead of recovering even
a fraction of it through a healthier, larger tax-paying workforce.
The 2021 State of Caring survey found many carers are already in financial
distress, less satisfied with life, and critically don't feel valued.
This bill sends them a clear message: that they are a fiscal liability to be
legislated around, not workers deserving of basic employment rights.
Infometrics had some recommendations that point in the other direction completely.
Paid leave, tax credits, KiwiSaver contributions, increased carer support payments,
and access to the supported living payment.
They estimated that meaningful financial and well-being support for carers would
generate 20 to $40,000 per carer per year in
net national well-being benefit for comparatively modest public investment.
Schemes that were already in motion, such as Mana Whaikaha, enhanced individualized
funding, and Enabling Good Lives, were flagged as practical starting points for
exactly this kind of support. But it's worth the committee noting that this
government's track record with these very schemes is a cautionary one.
In March 2024, Whaikaha abruptly restricted what flexible funding could be used
for, citing a budget overspend with no consultation, cutting carers' access
to things like respite.
It took a sustained community consultation and nearly two years of hardship before
those restrictions are finally being unwound from April, with no apology
and no commitment to restoring what families lost in the meantime.
This is the same pattern that this bill risks repeating.
Cut first under fiscal pressure, consult only once the damage is already done.
This government has cut so many jobs and continues to squeeze the poor and disabled
to cover deficits created by giving tax breaks to landlords and tobacco companies.
I'm asking that the committee do not let this bill pass in its current form, not
only for the harm that it does to disabled whānau and their carers, but because
employing these carers properly is a net economic gain, not a
cost, at a time when the government should be creating jobs, not legislating
away the few legal pathways that carers have just won.
